title: Interaction Patterns & States
source: Starter KB compiled from trusted UX guidance and standards (NN/g, Material Design, Apple HIG, WCAG). Key refs: NN/g empty states + button states; Material errors/progress/snackbar/dialogs; Apple HIG loading/progress; WCAG Understanding 4.1.3 Status Messages.
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for designing and validating common interaction patterns and UI states (loading, empty, success, failure, partial success, permissions, offline). Helps teams choose the right feedback pattern, reduce user uncertainty, and improve recoverability and accessibility.
scope: Applies to user-facing interfaces across web and mobile: feedback, progress, notifications, dialogs, inline messaging, and state coverage for screens/components/flows. Excludes assistant behavior, tone control, output formatting rules, schema enforcement, and runtime tool/DS rules.
definitions: ### UI State
A distinct condition of a screen or component that affects what users see and can do (e.g., loading, empty, error, success, disabled).
### Loading State
A state where content or an operation is in progress; must communicate that the system is working and what the user should expect. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
### Determinate Progress
A progress indicator that shows measurable completion (e.g., progress bar with percent). Use when duration or completion can be estimated. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
### Indeterminate Progress
A progress indicator that shows activity without a completion estimate (e.g., spinner). Use when time is unknown but the system is working. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
### Skeleton State
A placeholder UI that approximates the final layout while content loads, improving perceived performance and reducing layout shift.
### Empty State
A screen/region with no data to show (first use, deleted content, unavailable content). Should explain what’s happening and provide a next step. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
### Zero Results State
A special empty state triggered by a query/filter returning no matches; should suggest how to broaden or change the query.
### Error State
A state indicating failure (validation, network, server, permission, conflict). Must support recovery when possible. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
### Success State
A state that confirms an action completed and indicates what changed (saved, submitted, sent).
### Status Message
An update about success/results, waiting state, progress, or errors that should be programmatically available to assistive tech without forcing a context change. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
### Snackbar / Toast
A transient message used for brief feedback that doesn’t block the user; may include a simple action (e.g., Undo). :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}
### Dialog
A blocking overlay requiring user attention/decision; use for critical confirmations, destructive actions, or complex decisions. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}
rulesConstraints: Always cover core states for key screens/flows: default/content, loading, empty/zero, error, success/confirmation, disabled, and offline/degraded where relevant.
Use feedback to reduce uncertainty: users should know what is happening, whether their action worked, and what to do next. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}
Choose the least disruptive pattern that still prevents harm: prefer inline feedback or snackbar over modal dialogs unless the decision is high-risk. :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}
Loading: show an indicator quickly when the user might perceive a stall; use skeletons when content layout is known and load time is noticeable. :contentReference[oaicite:11]{index=11}
Use determinate progress when you can estimate completion; otherwise use indeterminate activity indicators. :contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}
Empty states should (1) explain why it’s empty, (2) provide learning cues when appropriate, and (3) offer a clear next step. :contentReference[oaicite:13]{index=13}
Error states: if recovery is possible, provide a user action (retry, fix input, change filters); do not clear user input on failure. :contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}
For transient notifications, ensure critical information is not lost: persist when necessary or provide a durable history/log for important events.

Accessibility: ensure status messages (success, saving, loading, errors) are available to assistive technologies without forcing focus changes; do not rely on color alone. :contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}
Interaction states must be distinguishable (enabled, disabled, hover, focus, pressed) and consistent across components. :contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}

do: Use inline messaging for field-level or component-level issues; use page-level messaging for global failures affecting the whole screen.
Use snackbars/toasts for lightweight confirmations and reversible actions (e.g., Undo). :contentReference[oaicite:17]{index=17}
Use dialogs for destructive/irreversible actions or complex decisions that require explicit confirmation. :contentReference[oaicite:18]{index=18}
Provide clear success feedback for important actions (save, submit, send) and clarify what changed.
For empty-first-run experiences, provide a primary CTA to create/add/import and a brief explanation of value. :contentReference[oaicite:19]{index=19}
For zero results, suggest adjustments (remove filters, broaden search, check spelling).
Preserve user context on failures: keep form inputs, selections, and scroll position when reasonable.
Design partial success explicitly: show what completed, what failed, and what to do next.
Use consistent visual state patterns for interactive components (hover, focus, pressed, disabled). :contentReference[oaicite:20]{index=20}
Ensure status messages and confirmations are accessible (screen-reader discoverable) and not color-only. :contentReference[oaicite:21]{index=21}
dont: Don’t show blocking dialogs for low-risk confirmations (creates interruption and habituation). :contentReference[oaicite:22]{index=22}
Don’t use spinners for long, predictable operations when a determinate indicator is possible. :contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}
Don’t use empty states as dead ends (blank with no explanation or next step). :contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}
Don’t hide errors behind generic messages without recovery guidance. :contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}
Don’t clear user-entered data after an error unless the user explicitly resets it.
Don’t rely on color alone to indicate selection, errors, or status. :contentReference[oaicite:26]{index=26}
Don’t make critical status changes ephemeral with no way to find them again (e.g., disappearing toast for a failed payment).

examples: If a feed takes > ~1s: show skeleton rows matching the final layout. If the duration is unknown: show an indeterminate spinner with brief text like “Loading results…”. :contentReference[oaicite:27]{index=27}
Inline status near the control: “Saving…” then “Saved”. Avoid blocking the user unless there is a conflict risk. :contentReference[oaicite:28]{index=28}
After deleting an item: snackbar “Item deleted” + action “Undo”. :contentReference[oaicite:29]{index=29}
Inline banner: “We couldn’t load your dashboard. Check your connection and try again.” Button: “Retry”. :contentReference[oaicite:30]{index=30}
“No projects yet. Create your first project to organize your work.” Primary action: “Create project”. :contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31}
“No results for ‘knee brace’.” Suggestions: “Remove filters” / “Search for a broader term” / “Check spelling”.
“You don’t have access to this report.” Next steps: “Request access” or “Switch account”. Provide a non-blocking path to continue elsewhere.
edgeCases: Offline/degraded mode: clearly indicate offline state, preserve user input, and clarify whether actions are queued or must be retried.
Long-running operations: show progress, allow safe cancellation when possible, and avoid freezing UI; use determinate progress when feasible. :contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}
Rate limits or repeated failures: after multiple retries, change the guidance (wait, try later, contact support) and avoid infinite retry loops.
Conflicts (simultaneous edits): explain what changed, show differences if possible, and offer a resolution path (keep mine / keep theirs).
Multi-pane/complex apps: empty states may be local to a panel; provide local next steps without hijacking the entire screen. :contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}
Accessibility: status messages that don’t move focus must still be announced to assistive tech and remain discoverable long enough to be understood. :contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}
Security-sensitive actions: require stronger confirmation only when the action is destructive or irreversible; otherwise prefer undo.
